entities.name: Snap
entities.how_described: 本集讨论的核心公司。旗下 Snapchat 拥有十亿月活、年营收超 60 亿美元；本集讲述它 15 年来的创新史、面临的护城河挑战、在硬件上的持续押注，以及称之为「严峻考验时刻」的当下转折点。

evidence.quote: I feel like Snap has always been punching above its weight in terms of just how much new stuff comes out of your team, stories, AR glasses, swipe-based navigation.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: Today, my guest is Evan Spiegel, CEO and co-founder of Snap.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: So I think in many ways it's a blessing that we've been able to continue to innovate to make new things that really resonate with folks and that ultimately people want to end up copying from Snap.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: One of the first things we did was focus a lot on building ecosystems, whether those are the relationships between creators and Snap-chatters, whether that's the platform we built around augmented reality where developers have built millions of these lenses.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: I feel like Snap has always been punching above its weight in terms of just how much new stuff comes out of your team, how many inventions happen considering the size of the company and the history.

evidence.quote: And actually when I read it, I was like, "Oh my gosh, that kind of explains a lot of how we think about innovation at Snap."
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And I think to summarize the academic version, we can talk about what we've developed and built at Snap, but to summarize, essentially his findings are that basically if you want to deliver a product at scale, you usually need quite a large organization.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And so when I read that, I was like, wow, that actually sounds quite similar to what we have at Snap.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And I think the beauty of what I love about Snap is that the dialogue between those two teams is where a lot of that innovation happens because a lot of our engineers working to serve our customers or working on reliability have a bunch of great ideas and so do our designers.

evidence.quote: I mean, I think it's an important part of it, but a lot of how we built Snap was modeled actually on mine and Bobby's original relationship, right?
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And one of the things we invented that at the time, we figured out a way, we realized that if you were pressing and holding to open a snap and left your finger on the screen and you took a screenshot, it would basically trigger an event on the phone essentially that made ...
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: Something that is also really, I'd say famous in the PM community about Snap is you guys waited a long time to hire product managers.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: I'm curious if you think that was a mistake waiting that long and just your current thoughts on product managers, just like how they fit into Snap.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: And I absolutely did not want that type of culture to take root because again, as I mentioned, we modeled a lot of the way that we develop products here at Snap on that early relationship between myself and Bobby, really this harmonious relationship between design and engineering and how important that is, that dialogue is to building great things and great products.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And that's a really big and really meaningful change in terms of the way that the design works here at Snap and the way the creative process works.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And I think for us at Snap, having design really actually be that bottleneck in terms of helping to manage what we're shipping and what it looks like and how it all works together, I think is really important.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And so I think for me, in addition to all the great people I get to work with here at Snap, the thing that wakes me up with a smile on my face every morning, the reason why I'm skipping to work is because I am in the weeds on what we're building and shipping to our community.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So as I was preparing for this chat, I talked to a bunch of people that worked at Snap over the years and I've integrated their ideas throughout already.

entities.name: Snapchat
entities.how_described: 15 年前发布的持久消费者社交应用，本集说它当初的崛起靠的是连接用户「最在乎的少数人」(亲密关系),而非像传统社交网络那样追求把所有人连起来。

evidence.quote: In the 15 years since Snapchat launched, there are essentially zero social consumer apps that have launched and stuck around.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: Snapchat has over one billion monthly active users, is generating over $6 billion a year in revenue.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: People post over eight billion AR lens photos a day on Snapchat.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: Basically, nothing else has worked other than Snapchat 15 years ago.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: We were so fortunate when we created Snapchat, the mobile phone and the app store were just getting started.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: Instagram, I think it started a year before Snapchat or something like that.

evidence.quote: So as I reflect on the early days of building Snapchat, the thing that we figured out in terms of distribution, especially when it comes to social, was that back then people believed that there were network effects and social networks that meant that the more people you had using the service, the stickier that it is.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And what Snapchat figured out that was, I think, different was that despite the fact that there were much bigger networks that connected more people, what really mattered was connecting you to the right people.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: You think about a lot of the big consumer technology companies today, they were really born out of mobile, whether it's Uber, Snapchat, some of those early generation companies that really got distribution and built themselves on mobile.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: Something that has always been really interesting to me about Snapchat is it feels like you guys have just innovated so much over the years.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: Yeah, I think we just hit 25 million subscribers on Snapchat plus more than a billion revenue run rate.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And that's something that we realized early on with Snapchat.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: It's so annoying to select everybody on my list of friends and Snapchat.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: I actually love the way that Snapchat allows me to just express myself and share with my friends without that sort of judgment and pressure.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: It was iterative because in a lot of ways, some of the early ideas started more around a status update that was sort of like, how could you know what your friends on Snapchat were up to even if you weren't sending Snaps back and forth?
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: One of my favorite stories was around screenshot detection actually in the early days because when we first built Snapchat and we shared with people, "Oh, you could send photos that disappear."
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And I think that that early invention was one of the first things that really got a lot of traction in the early days and I think helped Snapchat spread as a new way of communicating.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: I can't imagine Snapchat existing without you figuring that out.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: And so I think removing friction from the creative process and being able to go from idea to impact at the scale of almost a billion people using Snapchat is really empowering and exciting for designers who feel like these tools maybe level the playing field to some extent for creative people.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: But I think because I really like leading our company and leading the direction and strategy for the business, and because I see a huge opportunity in transforming the way that people use computing in the future, both through Snapchat, but also with specs, I think this is the right role for me right now.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And so I think this is the year that we have to prove that Snapchat can be a really strong, profitable business, that it continues to grow both in terms of the reach of our audience and their engagement with new products, whether it's topic chats or spotlight, or I think we've got 200 million people playing games every month on Snapchat now.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So gaming is becoming a big part of the engagement drivers on Snapchat.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

entities.name: Specs

evidence.quote: But I think because I really like leading our company and leading the direction and strategy for the business, and because I see a huge opportunity in transforming the way that people use computing in the future, both through Snapchat, but also with specs, I think this is the right role for me right now.

entities.name: 分发 (distribution)
entities.how_described: 本集说它是今天消费者技术里最难搞清楚的事,也是新社交产品几乎全军覆没的根因;主持人判断:AI 让做产品的门槛降低,而「分发」将成为 AI 帮不上忙的新护城河。

evidence.quote: People don't spend nearly enough time thinking about distribution and figuring out distribution.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: Well, I think it's really interesting in terms of the examples that you just shared of TikTok and threads, because you just shared two examples of people who figured out distribution.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: It's a lot harder to get distribution for new ideas and new services.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And both TikTok and Threads figured out distribution, which is why I think there are more recent examples of success.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And I think with Threads, obviously, they were able to leverage the amazing distribution that Meta has across all of their other products.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And I think people don't spend nearly enough time thinking about distribution and figuring out distribution.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So as I reflect on the early days of building Snapchat, the thing that we figured out in terms of distribution, especially when it comes to social, was that back then people believed that there were network effects and social networks that meant that the more people you had using the service, the stickier that it is.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: We had the benefit of the app store and mobile distribution, but we also had a different way of growing our service, which wasn't about having the most friends.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: It's something that I've been thinking a lot about actually, this idea of distribution, because of AI.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: And I feel like distribution is where it ends up being, what ends up being the new moat and the new biggest challenge because AI is not going to really help you there.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: You think about a lot of the big consumer technology companies today, they were really born out of mobile, whether it's Uber, Snapchat, some of those early generation companies that really got distribution and built themselves on mobile.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: Especially if they have distribution already.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: It's going to be very hard to get distribution just like there's so much launching every day, so much to pay attention to.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

entities.name: 护城河 (moat)
entities.how_described: 本集反复讨论的业务壁垒概念。Snap 在 15 年前就领悟「软件不是护城河」;为对抗功能被克隆,他们转而构建难复制的生态/平台,并押注更难复制的硬件。

evidence.quote: 15 years ago, we essentially learned that software is not a moat, which is something that everyone is discovering today with AI.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And I feel like distribution is where it ends up being, what ends up being the new moat and the new biggest challenge because AI is not going to really help you there.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: And so I think one of the things, it's been an interesting time for me to reflect a lot on our strategy over the last 15 years, because 15 years ago, we essentially learned that software is not a moat, which is something that everyone is discovering today with AI.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: You would think network effects would be the moat.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: That's what everybody's always trying to get to because that's classically a great moat.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: Are you saying that that's maybe not even as great a moat or that's just not enough and then you need something more like say hardware?
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

entities.name: 网络效应 (network effects)
entities.how_described: 传统观念认为社交网络的价值在于网络效应(人越多越有价值);本集说 Snapchat 发现真正重要的不是连所有人、而是连最在乎的少数人,这是他们早期增长的引擎。

evidence.quote: So as I reflect on the early days of building Snapchat, the thing that we figured out in terms of distribution, especially when it comes to social, was that back then people believed that there were network effects and social networks that meant that the more people you had using the service, the stickier that it is.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: You would think network effects would be the moat.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: I think network effects are certainly an important piece of the puzzle for sure, but I don't think they're enough when it comes to these sorts of software cloning.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And so I think in addition to network effects, it's really important to try to build businesses that are more defensible by truly building out a platform that can support relationships between creators, developers, and the community, because that in and of itself we found is incredibly difficult to copy.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

entities.name: 增强现实 (augmented reality)
entities.how_described: 本集把它说成一种能把数字内容锚定在真实世界、让人不再低头看手机的技术;Snap 围绕它建了由开发者贡献数百万滤镜的生态,并即将发布全新 AR 眼镜 Specs。

evidence.quote: One of the first things we did was focus a lot on building ecosystems, whether those are the relationships between creators and Snap-chatters, whether that's the platform we built around augmented reality where developers have built millions of these lenses.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And then it also informed a lot of our thinking about investing in other places that are really hard to copy, including hardware, where it's really, really challenging to copy our fully vertically integrated stack around augmented reality.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: We were innovating, building lenses and augmented reality, but ultimately we saw that people were using AR on these tiny little phone screens that required you to use your thumbs to interact.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: We have the biggest augmented reality platform.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

entities.name: 智能体 (agents)
entities.how_described: 本集说 Snap 围绕社区与广告主的核心任务来配智能体:比如内部 App 摇一摇报错后有智能体自动调试并建议修复;一个产品想法传入「上市智能体」就能自动写规格、拉审批、做风险分析甚至写发布博客。

evidence.quote: We have a system at Snap where on the internal version of the app, you can shake to report your problem and agents now debug exactly what happened, what went wrong and can actually suggest a fix.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And so by listing out all these jobs to be done, really for the community journey and for advertisers as well, it became very clear where we could use agents, where we needed to be very focused in terms of building cross-functional teams around those jobs supported by AI tools.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So I think for us trying to, while I think in this moment of time, you certainly want to have a thousand flowers bloom and people are building agents and experimenting, I think at the same time, making sure that we stay focused on what matters to our community, what matters to advertisers is really, really important.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So I think there's all sorts of examples like that across the company in terms of the way that agents can play a really massive role in changing the way that we work.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

entities.name: 硬件 (hardware)
entities.how_described: 本集把它说成 Snap 最反直觉、也最难被抄袭的业务壁垒;做硬件的动机是造一种能把人从屏幕前解放出来、带回朋友身边的新型计算机。

evidence.quote: And then it also informed a lot of our thinking about investing in other places that are really hard to copy, including hardware, where it's really, really challenging to copy our fully vertically integrated stack around augmented reality.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And I was going to get to this later, but we can talk about this now, this big investment you've been putting into hardware.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: Are you saying that that's maybe not even as great a moat or that's just not enough and then you need something more like say hardware?
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: If you think about it, you're working on two of the hardest types of businesses, a consumer social platform and hardware.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: And this hardware investment, you've been at it for a long time.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: We're going to talk about it, but just maybe you've already answered it, why this is something you're so passionate about, but why this kind of ongoing investment in hardware?
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

entities.name: 要完成的任务 (jobs to be done)
entities.how_described: 本集说 Snap 用它来重新梳理整个公司运作:列出社区和广告商要完成的所有核心任务,据此构建跨职能团队并配以智能体,从而追踪每项任务对应的业务结果。

evidence.quote: So getting really, really basic and straightforward on, okay, for Snap-chatters, our jobs to be done, it's as simple as like get people to download the app, get them to add their close friends, get them to, I don't know, use lenses, something like that.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And so by listing out all these jobs to be done, really for the community journey and for advertisers as well, it became very clear where we could use agents, where we needed to be very focused in terms of building cross-functional teams around those jobs supported by AI tools.
